name: Lemuria Continent
description: Lemuria, also known as Mu, is a hypothetical lost continent proposed to have existed in the Pacific Ocean. Proponents suggest it was home to an advanced civilization predating known history, acting as a bridge between continents and explaining similarities in flora, fauna, and cultural practices across the Pacific region, including Polynesia, Micronesia, and Melanesia. The theory gained traction in the 19th century through the works of Augustus Le Plongeon and James Churchward, who cited sunken ruins and ancient texts as evidence. Churchward particularly popularized the concept through his books, describing Lemuria as a vast, peaceful civilization destroyed by cataclysmic events. Advocates point to the distribution of lemurs (though their actual distribution doesn't align with the proposed continent), similar megalithic structures across the Pacific, and shared cultural motifs in mythology as supporting evidence. Mainstream science dismisses Lemuria as pseudoscience. Plate tectonics demonstrates that a large continent could not have existed and sunk in the Pacific as described. The distribution of species and cultural similarities are better explained by migration, cultural diffusion, and convergent evolution. The 'ancient texts' cited are often misinterpreted or lack credible historical basis. The geological record provides no evidence of a submerged continent of the scale proposed by Lemurian theorists. The concept continues to resonate within esoteric and New Age circles, often linked to spiritual enlightenment and ancient wisdom.
subtitle: Hypothetical lost Pacific continent.

claims.claim: Lemuria was a real continent in the Pacific Ocean.
claims.evidence: Distribution of lemurs, similar megalithic structures across the Pacific, and shared cultural motifs.
claims.criticism: Plate tectonics disproves the existence of a large submerged continent. Species distribution and cultural similarities are better explained by migration and cultural diffusion.

claims.claim: Lemuria was home to an advanced, peaceful civilization.
claims.evidence: Interpretation of ancient texts and supposed sunken ruins.
claims.criticism: The 'ancient texts' are often misinterpreted or lack credible historical basis. No verifiable sunken ruins have been found.

claims.claim: Lemuria's destruction caused widespread flooding and influenced the development of other cultures.
claims.evidence: Mythological flood narratives and perceived similarities between different cultures.
claims.criticism: Flood myths are common across many cultures and likely arose independently. Cultural similarities are better explained through trade and migration rather than a single source.

keyFigures.name: James Churchward
keyFigures.role: Popularized the Lemuria theory through his books.

keyFigures.name: Augustus Le Plongeon
keyFigures.role: Early proponent who linked Lemuria to Mayan civilization.

keyFigures.name: Charles Darwin
keyFigures.role: His theory of evolution and biogeography are used to explain species distribution without needing a land bridge.

timeline.event: Theosophical Society promotes the idea of Lemuria.
James Churchward popularizes Lemuria through his books.
Lemuria remains a popular topic in New Age and esoteric circles.

sources.author: L. Sprague de Camp
sources.work: Lost Continents: The Atlantis Theme in History, Science, and Literature
sources.title: L. Sprague de Camp

sources.work: The Story of Atlantis and the Lost Lemuria
sources.title: W. Scott-Elliot
